coredata.dc:title: High-sensitivity detection for cantharidin by ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on molecularly imprinted nanoparticles of quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ling, Jiang
Zhang, Wenqi
Cheng, Zijia
Ding, Yanjun
coredata.dc:description: Cantharidin (CTD), an effective component of Chinese herbal medicine with unique efficacy, caused poisoning or death constantly for incorrect use. Therefore, there is an intense need for a reliable, selective, sensitive and low-costing analytical method for monitoring the concentration of CTD in biological samples. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor (CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs) was successfully constructed for selective determination of CTD by simply integrated the non-imprinted polymers coated CDs (CDs@NIPs) with cadmium telluride quantum dots coated with molecular imprinted polymers (CdTe@MIPs). CDs@NIPs were directly used as reference signal without addition of CTD templates and the as-prepared CdTe@MIPs were response signal. With the CTD addition, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe@MIPs decreased, while the fluorescence of CDs@NIPs remained approximately unchanged, resulting a detection limit as low as 0.15 nM. By spiked human blood samples, the ratiometric sensor was successfully applied to CTD detection with good recoveries of 96.12–107.40% and relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.87–3.96%. Moreover, after centrifuging and re-eluting, the developed sensor could be effectively recycled and reused five times. Significantly, the proposed platform with low-cost and high-sensitivity opens a door towards the practical applications of ratiometric sensor and shows immense potential in pursuing the sensitive, reliable and low-cost determination of poisons in forensic toxicology analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chinese herbal medicine
Cantharidin
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Imprinted polymers
Cadmium telluride quantum dots
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Proposed detection mechanism of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor
Calibration curve and selectivity of CTD

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A: UV–vis absorption spectrum of (a) CTD, (b) CdTe@MIPs and fluorescence spectrum of (c) CdTe@MIPs. B: Schematic of the fluorescence quenching mechanism on the basis of the electron-transfer process (a) and molecular orbital theory (b).
TEM images of (A) CdTe@MIPs, (B) CDs@NIPs, (C) CDs and (D) CdTe.

Fluorescent spectra of CdTe@MIPs, CDs@NIPs, CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs and eluant of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor. The symmetric emission peaks at 680 nm and 520 nm of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs exactly matched with that of CdTe@MIPs and CDs@NIPs.
(A) FT-IR spectra of (a) CDs@NIPs, (b) CdTe@MIPs after elution and (c) CdTe@MIPs before elution, (B) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of CdTe@MIPs.
The fluorescence quenching capacities or intensities of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor for CTD under different conditions. A: pH, B: temperature, C: incubation time, D: photoluminescence stability.

Fluorescence spectra of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs with addition different concentrations of CTD (0.5, 5, 20, 80, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1000 nM). The insert shows a good linear relationship between ratio of I 520/I 680 with the concentration of CTD (CCTD).
Fluorescence spectra of CdTe@NIPs/CDs@NIPs with addition different concentrations of CTD (0.5, 5, 20, 80, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1000 nM).

Selectivity of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor for CTD. The fluorescence intensities at 520 nm (I 520) and 680 nm (I 680) were recorded after addition of 1000 nM of CTD, tetrodotoxin, bufotoxin, bee venom, glucose, urea, uric acid, creatine, creatinine, cholesterol, phospholipid, and Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, respectively.
Fluorescence intensities of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor at 680 nm before (upside) and after (downside) addition of 1000 nM CTD with reused times.

Main procedure of the molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent sensor-based detection method (CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs) for cantharidin (CTD) in blood, including the recycle and reusage of the sensor after centrifugation and re-eluting.
Evaluating the tolerance limit of interference during detection of 1000 nM CTD by CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs.

row.entry: Tolerance limit for ions in blood (mM)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of determination performance of CTD in blood samples by the developed CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor and LC–MS/MS.
Comparison of performance with different methods.

head.ce:title: High-sensitivity detection for cantharidin by ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on molecularly imprinted nanoparticles of quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Forensic Science, School of Basic Medical Science, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Forensic Science, School of Basic Medical Science, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cantharidin (CTD), an effective component of Chinese herbal medicine with unique efficacy, caused poisoning or death constantly for incorrect use. Therefore, there is an intense need for a reliable, selective, sensitive and low-costing analytical method for monitoring the concentration of CTD in biological samples. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor (CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs) was successfully constructed for selective determination of CTD by simply integrated the non-imprinted polymers coated CDs (CDs@NIPs) with cadmium telluride quantum dots coated with molecular imprinted polymers (CdTe@MIPs). CDs@NIPs were directly used as reference signal without addition of CTD templates and the as-prepared CdTe@MIPs were response signal. With the CTD addition, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe@MIPs decreased, while the fluorescence of CDs@NIPs remained approximately unchanged, resulting a detection limit as low as 0.15 nM. By spiked human blood samples, the ratiometric sensor was successfully applied to CTD detection with good recoveries of 96.12–107.40% and relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.87–3.96%. Moreover, after centrifuging and re-eluting, the developed sensor could be effectively recycled and reused five times. Significantly, the proposed platform with low-cost and high-sensitivity opens a door towards the practical applications of ratiometric sensor and shows immense potential in pursuing the sensitive, reliable and low-cost determination of poisons in forensic toxicology analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Mylabris, an ancient traditional Chinese medicine for more than 2000 years, is the dry body of Mylabris phalerata Pallas or Mylabris cichorii Linnaeus [1]. According to related records in Chinese medical books, Mylabris has biological action of eroding mycosis, dispersing obstructions and lumps, removing blood stasis and eliminating toxic material, and thus used to treat such diseases as carbuncle, boils, ulcer, and mycosis. Modern researches indicated that cantharidin (CTD) (3,6-epoxy-1,2-dimethylcyclohexane-1,2-dicarboxylic anhydride) was one of the active ingredients of mylabris [2] and showed particular therapeutic efficacy in the treatments of cancer and some refractory diseases, immunity regulating and anti-inflammatory effects [3]. And now, cantharidin-containing Chinese medicine preparations have been widely used in clinic [4,5].

However, as we know, different processing methods and the usage of raw mylabris in the treatment were lack of unified clinical efficacy and safety evaluation standard for CTD [6,7]. The potential poisoning risk of liver or kidney injury and gastrointestinal toxicity caused by unreasonable or improper use of the drugs in patients far exceed the benefit of therapeutic effect [8,9]. In view of this, cases of poisoning or even death due to multiple organ failure caused by mylabris were reported in forensic medicine [10–12] from time to time. For now, the determination of CTD intoxication of sufferers was performed by gas chromatography (GC) [13], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [15,16] and liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) [17,18]. Among which, GC, HPLC and GC–MS were hard to satisfy the detection of trace CTD in biological samples for poor sensitivity. In comparison, method of LC–MS/MS proved high sensitivity with detection limit about nanogram per milliliter and showed great advantages in determination of trace-level poisons in blood. However, the high-cost instrument of LC–MS/MS which was located in laboratory and operated by professional technicians restrict the broad applications.

Recently, fluorescence sensor or fluorimetry has been successfully applied to determination of poisons because of their superiorities such as simple operation, rapid response, high sensitivity and low-cost [19–23]. Especially, molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) [24–28], an emerging detection technique for target recognition and analysis based on memory function of the size, shape and functional groups of module molecule, were popular to unite with fluorescent signal materials (such as CDs, CdTe) [29,30] to construct sensors. Traditionally, the MIPs sensors were prepared by incorporating fluorescent substances and temple into imprinting matrix via one-pot. The site accessibility and mass transfer were vastly restrained by imprinted cavities which deeply-located in the highly-crosslinked MIPs. In addition, the unevenly distribution of fluorescent substances leading to difficulty signal response to the recombination of trace targets, which reduce the sensitivity of the sensor and limit its application and development [31,32]. Hence, the surface imprinting strategy was developed to optimize such defects and attracted extensive attention. With water-compatible capability, high sensitivity and facile synthesis process, sol–gel process (e.g. Stӧber method) using tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) as crosslinker and functional monomer, respectively, was favored by researchers and became the most popular surface imprinting strategy to construct sensors [33,34]. After elution, the sensors offer amounts of specific recognition cavities to identify target molecules and are better options than antibodies that with a limited number of sites. While, most current MIPs sensors were based on single wavelength fluorescence response, and can be easily disturbed by environmental conditions and instrument efficiency, which make output short of sufficient repeatability. Significantly, compared with single signal fluorescence imprinting sensor, the improved ratiometric fluorescence sensor possesses self-referencing ability with double-signal [35–37], which would eliminate the above unwanted effects as much as possible to improve sensitivity and accuracy. For example, Han et al. [35] reported a dual-response ratiometric fluorescence sensor which was fabricated by chelating europium ions (Eu3+) onto cadmium telluride quantum dots for real-time and visible detection of tetracycline. Guo’s group [38] designed a ratiometric fluorescent probe for doxycycline detection by using S dots and calcium ion. The above mentioned ratiometric fluorescence sensors were strikingly enhance selectivity and accuracy. Thus, establishing a low-cost ratiometric fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive and accurate detection of targets especially CTD would make incredible progress in toxicological analysis.

With this goal in mind, a surface molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on CDs and CdTe for sensitive detection of CTD was firstly designed and showed in Scheme 1 . In the dual-emission sensor, CdTe coated with MIPs (CdTe@MIPs) was synthesized via sol–gel process with CTD template. The fluorescence of CdTe@MIPs was quenched due to the electron transfer between CTD molecular and the CdTe@MIPs which were covered with specific recognition caves. Moreover, the non-imprinted polymers coated on CDs (CDs@NIPs) as a reference signal were also prepared without addition of CTD template. After coated SiO2 layer on CDs@NIPs surface, the CDs@NIPs were scarcely respond to CTD for lacking specific recognition function and the fluorescence signal maintained stability. Hence, a ratiometric sensor with outstanding performances such as low-costing, highly sensitive and accuracy was successfully developed by integrating CdTe@MIPs with CDs@NIPs and it could be effectively recycled and reused after centrifugation and re-eluting, providing a facile way for CTD determination. To our knowledge, ratiometric fluorescent MIP based on opto-sensing materials that can directly, sensitively, and selectively detecting CTD in biological samples has never been reported up to now.

3-(3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), o-Phenylenediamine (oPD), ethanol, acetic acid, acetonitrile, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), absolute ethanol, isopropyl alcohol, tellurium powder, cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), formic acid, CaCl2, NaCl, MgCl2, and glucose, urea, uric acid, creatine, creatinine, cholesterol, phospholipid and ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The standard substances of cantharidin, tetrodotoxin, bufotoxin, bee venom (≧99.5%, UPLC) were purchased from Beijing Jin Bao Online Technology Co., LTD. All chemicals commercially available are of analytical grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm, Millipore).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded by F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were performed on 850 spectrophotometer (Tianjin Gangdong SCI.&TECH. CO,.LTD). UV–Vis spectra were measured with Agilent Cary 60 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging was performed with HT7800 Hitachi TEM system at 300 kV. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) methods were used to analyse the surface areas and volume pore from nitrogen sorption using ASAP 2460 with the out-gassing temperature at 150 °C. Amicon Ultra-4centrifugal filter unit with 10 kDa MW cut-off was used to purify the CDs.

LC–MS–MS conditions were as follows: mobile phase: 20 mmol ammonium acetate in acetonitrile and 0.1% formic acid in deionized water (70:30). Separation performed on a Zorbax Eclipse Plus C18 column (150 mm × 2.1 mm, 3.5 µm) (Agilent, America). Injection volume: 5 µL. Elution was performed at a flow rate gradient of 0.25 mL/min for 10 min with the column oven temperature was set to 40 °C. Mass spectrometric conditions: electrospray ionization positive ion mode (ESI+). Vcap: 4500 v; drying gas: nitrogen with flow rate at 10 L/min, temperature 500 °C; Collision gas: Helium, collision energy: 10/22 v. Declustering potential: 89 V for CTD. Precursor/product ion: 197.085/95.207 (CE: 17 eV), 197.085/123.139 (CE: 14 eV) for CTD.

The route for preparation of CdTe@MIPs was as follows. CdTe-QDs were synthesized according to previous report with some modifications at first [39]. After that, 0.5 mg CTD and 20 μL APTES were dissolved in 2 mL ethanol and stirred about 10 min. 2 mL CdTe-QDs solution, 20 μL TEOS and 20 µL NH3·H2O were then added and continuously stirred for 10 min. The products, CdTe@MIPs, were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min and washed with ethanol/acetic acid (90:10, v/v) until no templates were detected from the washing solvent by UV vis spectrophotometer, and then dried for 2 h in oven at 60 °C. As a control, CdTe-QDs@non-molecularly imprinted polymers (CdTe@NIPs) were prepared with the same procedures as those for CdTe@MIPs but in the absence of CTD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method according to the previous report [40]. After that, the CDs@NIPs were synthesized followed the above section of preparation of CdTe@MIPs beyond the addition of CTD. 0.5 mL CDs were added into 5 mL ethanol/water (v:v, 4:1) and treated with ultrasound 20 min. After that, 10 µL APTES and 20 µL TEOS were added in the mixed solution. Then polymerization was initiated by the addition of 20 µL NH3·H2O, the whole process was under continuous shaking and persisted for 12 h. The resultant particles were washed by ethanol/acetic acid (90:10, v/v) and water with ultrasound several times and dried in oven at 60 °C for 2 h. Finally, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs was constructed by physically integrating 1 mg CDs@NIPs with 1 mg CdTe@MIPs in 5 mL ethanol and regarded as working solution. In addition, CdTe@NIPs/CDs@NIPs have been prepared in the same way for comparison.

In short, 0.1 mL of CTD with different concentrations of 0.5, 5, 20, 80, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1000 nM were added into 0.5 mL of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor working solution, respectively. After fully mixed and incubated in 200 rpm for 8 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra of the sensor were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer in a 1 × 1 cm quartz cuvette with an emission range of 470–710 nm. Fluorescence intensities were monitored at 520 nm (I 520) and 680 nm (I 680). Finally, the fluorescence ratio (I 520/I 680) and the concentration of CTD were used as ordinate and abscissa respectively to investigate the calibration curve of the sensor. Besides, the blank comparison experiments were conducted by CdTe@NIPs/CDs@NIPs. The fluorescence spectra were carried out under the following conditions: excitation wavelength, 370 nm; excitation and emission slit width, 10 nm; photomultiplier tube voltage, 700 V. All the samples were measured for three times. Fluorescence measurements were recorded after all the reactions were completed.

In order to investigate the selectivity of the ratiometric sensor, effects of common interferents on the method were explored, including tetrodotoxin, bufotoxin, bee venom, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, and glucose, urea, uric acid, creatine, creatinine, cholesterol, phospholipid etc.

For blood sample analysis, blank human blood samples were collected from healthy laboratory volunteers. Subsequently, the samples were spiked with different amounts of CTD with final concentrations of 50 nM, 100 nM and 500 nM, respectively. 2 mL of acetonitrile was added into 500 μL of blood to remove proteins in blood. After shaken vigorously for 5 min, the mixture was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant was filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove large particles. The obtained supernatant was concentrated to dry under nitrogen, then 100 μL of ethanol was added and redissolved the residual for detection according to the procedure in Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed detection mechanism of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: With chemically inert, rigid structure and hydrophilic capability, silica was an ideal material for molecular imprinting [41]. CdTe@MIPs and CDs@NIPs were synthesized respectively by one-pot sol–gel polymerization based on Stӧber method to construct CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs ratiometric sensor. Among which, CdTe@MIPs were employed as fluorescent response signal and another part with invariable fluorescence was reference signal. The silica nanospheres were fabricated via the hydrolysis and condensation reaction of TEOS and APTES. Combining the CdTe with functional monomer APTES and functional group of CTD (carbonyl) via non-covalent interactions or reversible covalent bonds (such as van der Waals forces, ionic interactions, hydrogen bonding), the polymer (CdTe@MIPs) was formed under catalysis of NH3·H2O [42,43], and the CdTe@MIPs were developed by embedding CTD (template molecules) in the silica-coated matrix successfully. Consequently, after removing excess reagents and template molecules by elution, the cavities that exposed on the surface of CdTe@MIPs would specifically recognize and bond with CTD, triggering fluorescence response of the sensor.

According to previous literature report [30], the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching may attribute to energy transfer or charge transfer from CdTe@MIPs to analytes (CTD). As can be seen in Fig. 1 A, the emission wavelength of CdTe@MIPs was 680 nm, and the UV adsorption bands for CTD was 230 nm. Obviously, there was no spectral overlap between emission spectrum of CdTe-@MIPs and absorption spectrum of CTD, ruling out energy resonance transfer as the possible quenching mechanism. Moreover, even when the excitation wavelength was set at 400 nm, the fluorescence of CdTe@MIPs was also quenched by CTD, which indicated that the inner filter effect can not be used to explain the fluorescence quenching (CTD has no absorption to such excitation light) [44]. Therefore, this indicated that the charge transfer from CdTe@MIPs to CTD was responsible for the fluorescence quenching mechanism and the molecular orbital theory was illustrated in Fig. 1B. The electrons of CdTe with accepting ultraviolet photon were excited from the valence band to the conduction band, and then the excited electrons return to the ground state and emit red fluorescence in the absence of CTD. With the presence of CTD, the shift of charge between CdTe and CTD was generated by the strong interaction force of hydrogen bond. Moreover, there exists an overlap between the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CTD and CdTe@MIPs (Fig. 1A), which made the excited electron transfer into the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the complex followed the paths shown as the dashed arrows and leads to the fluorescence quenching [45–48]. However, as a reference signal, the CDs@NIPs synthesised under the same fabrication procedure of CdTe@MIPs except the templates addition were barely respond to CTD since they have been wrapped with silica and lack of specific cavities. Hence, when CdTe@MIPs were integrated with CDs@NIPs in appropriate proportion, the CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor showed dual-emission fluorescent peaks at 520 and 680 nm with excitation at 370 nm and accomplish determination of CTD with outstanding performances of high sensitivity and accuracy.

To conform the successful construction of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs, FT-IR spectra of CDs@NIPs, CdTe@MIPs before and after templates elution were investigated and shown in Fig. 4 A. For the three materials, peaks at 881 cm−1 corresponding to Si-O vibrations, and the strong and broad absorption peaks at about 1049 cm−1 suggested the existence of Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching. Moreover, the peak at 804 cm−1 of CdTe@MIPs before elution vanished in the spectrum of CdTe@MIPs after elution, and the phenomenon indicated the characteristic may attribute to the anhydride of CTD. Therefore, the presence of these peaks further manifested that the CTD was imprinted and MIPs were synthesized on the surface of CdTe or CDsMIPs successfully. Moreover, the surface area and the pore size of CdTe@MIPs were obtained from the N2 adsorption test and mathematical treatments by the BET and BJH equation (Fig. 4B). From Fig. 4B, the BET surface area of CdTe@MIPs was found to be 43.3214 m2/g and the average diameter of BJH pore was about 18 nm. Meanwhile, the existence of mesoporous pores can be verified from the isotherm curves [49].

In order to obtain high sensitivity and stable performance, various conditions including temperature, pH and response time, affecting the properties of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs were investigated and optimized (Fig. 5 ). The value of I 520/I 680 was regarded as indicator of quenching efficiency. I 680 and I 520 were represented the fluorescence emission intensities at 680 nm and 520 nm of the sensor, respectively.

The pH value of solution plays a very important role on the fluorescence response of sensing platform and the interactions between CTD and CdTe@MIPs. As shown in Fig. 5A, I 520/I 680 increased with pH increasing gradually in the range of 4.0–8.0 and decreases from 8.0 to 11.0. The quenching efficiency reached maximum with pH 8.0. Theoretically, the fluorescence of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs should be high under alkaline conditions because it was synthesized under the condition. However, CTD will be hydrolyzed with strong alkaline conditions which would damage the fluorescence response. Meanwhile, under highly alkaline conditions, the silica shell will be ionized which can damage the structure of the binding cavities and cause surface defects [50,51]. In addition, the –NH2 groups of APTES may be induced by positive charge (NH3+) which resulted in poor affinity for CTD when pH is lower than 7.0. Based on this, pH 8.0 was selected as optimum pH for further experiments.

To investigate the effects of temperature and incubation time on CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs, the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm and 680 nm were recorded at different conditions after addition of 1000 nM CTD. As can be seen in Fig. 5B and Fig. 5C respectively, the ratio value was stable with incubation temperature range from 10 °C to 40 °C, fluorescence response increased within 1–8 min and almost be stable beyond 8 min. Hence, the desired effect would be achieved with the sensor incubated for 8 min at room temperature, and subsequent experiments were carried out under this condition.

The photoluminescence stability of the sensor is an important indicator to consider the material would be used in practical detection. As seen in Fig. 5D, the fluorescence intensity (I 520 and I 680) does not fluctuate observably within 6 h which indicated that the ratiometric sensor possesses super fluorescence stability. The re-markable stability might come from the robust protection of silica-based MIPs shell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve and selectivity of CTD
ce:section.ce:para: The calibration curve and sensitivity performance of the CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor were investigated by adding CTD with different concentrations from 0 to 1000 nM (Fig. 6 ). As can be seen in Fig. 6, the fluorescence intensity at 680 nm of CdTe@MIPs quenched gradually while that of CDs@NIPs at 520 nm remained almost constant with augmented CTD concentration which resulting in the ratio of I 520/I 680 increased. Due to the response and stability, the fluorescence intensity ratio shows regular changes with the addition of CTD. In the range of 0–1000 nM, the linear relationship of I 520/I 680 and C CTD was I 520/I 680 = 2.6863 × 10−4 CCTD +0.5093 (R 2 = 0.9690), and the low detection limit (LOD) was estimated to be 0.15 nM, limit of quantitation (LOQ) was 0.5 nM (LOD = 3σ/S, LOQ = 10σ/S, σ was the standard deviation of blank samples, and S was the slope of the calibration curve). As shown in the Fig. 7 , the sensitivity of CdTe@NIPs/CDs@NIPs was also investigated. The fluorescence intensity at 680 nm and 520 nm decreased slightly or even negligible when adding the same amount of CTD compared with CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs. Thanks for abundant specific imprinting recognition sites on the CdTe@MIPs surface, CTD gets more opportunities to couple with the sensor and quench CdTe fluorescence. The quenching efficiency of CTD to CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs was much higher than that for CdTe@NIPs/CDs@NIPs, verified that CdTe-QDs@MIPs had imprinted cavities for specific recognition of CTD. Obviously, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on double emission of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs shows high sensitivity.

Feasibility and reproducibility are also important characteristic in the application of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs. To evaluate these performances in practical application, the developed ratiometric CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor was employed to the determination of CTD in human blood by standard addition. Firstly, blood samples donated by laboratory volunteer were spiked with different concentrations of CTD (50 nM, 100 nM and 500 nM). Then, these samples were tested systematically by the developed ratiometric sensor and calculated by calibration curve to investigate accuracy of this method. In the accuracy experiment, samples with different concentrations were paralleled three times to obtain intra-day precision and repeated for three consecutive days to obtain inter-day precision (Show in Table 2 ). Finally, the CTD concentration in these samples were verified by LC–MS/MS. As shown in Table 2, compelling recoveries of 96.12–107.40% with the intra-day and inter-day precision of the three groups were lower than 4%, 8%, respectively, for the spiked blood samples based on the spectrofluorometer determination. Moreover, the results in the table showed that the measurement results by CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor were highly matched with those of LC–MS/MS. Promisingly, the ratiometric sensor showed significant advantages of application and reliability for CTD determination.

Furthermore, removing-rebinding cycles were repeated to inspect the re-usability of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs. As shown in Fig. 9 , the sensor could be recycled by centrifugation and reused after re-eluting CTD; further stresses that quenching efficiency was barely significant effected within five repetitions and was greatly affected after the sixth repetition. The results indicated the CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs could be reused five times and cost-effective applications in real samples for CTD.

The analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the hybrid CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs optosensing platform was compared with other methods described in previous works (Table 3 ). At present, the reported detection methods of CTD were focused on GC-MS and LC/MSMS technology. To the best of our knowledge, sensor technology, especially ratiometric fluorescent sensing technology, has not been reported for CTD detection so far. As seen, the detection limit of headspace solid-phase microextraction (HS-SPME) coupled to gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) is slightly better than the CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs sensor’s. However, the cost of HS-SPME/GC–MS is too high, and the process of pretreatment is time-consuming and complex. In comparison, it was noteworthy that the sensor provided a superior LOD/LOQ in comparison with GC–MS and LC–MS/MS, which made the trace CTD can be sensitively recognized. Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescence system based on molecular imprinting was firstly used to CTD determination, which was simple, rapid, and cost-effective and demonstrates good selectivity.

In this work, a highly dosage-sensitive, reliable and low-cost CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs ratiometric sensor for determination of CTD was synthesized by a one-pot sol–gel process. In this dual-emission sensor, CdTe@MIPs were served as receptor and response unit for CTD, while CDs@NIPs as reference signal to improve stability. Taking advantages of ratiometric sensing, the sensor exhibits good linear range of 0-1000 nM with LOD of 0.15 nM and shows prominent selectivity for CTD over other analogues or interferents, including, tetrodotoxin, bufotoxin, bee venom, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, and, urea, uric acid, creatine, creatinine, cholesterol, phospholipid, glucose. More attractively, the sensor could be effectively reused five times after centrifuging and re-eluting. By spiked human blood samples, good recoveries of 96.12–107.40% with RSD of 2.87–3.96% demonstrated that the application of CdTe@MIPs/CDs@NIPs ratiometric sensor shows promising future in actual analysis of blood sample. In summary, it can be concluded that the designed recyclable ratiometric fluorescent sensor with low-costing, facile and high sensitivity owns a bright future for CTD determination and was expected to lay the foundation for the progress of poisons sensing in forensic toxicology analysis.
Jiang Ling: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Wenqi Zhang: Data curation. Zijia Cheng: Validation. Yanjun Ding: Validation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81772025).
